arguments.prompt:
Review all MSBuild project files (.csproj, .vbproj, .fsproj, .props, .targets, Directory.Build.props, Directory.Build.targets, Directory.Packages.props) in this .NET solution rooted at /tmp/sv-649df186943e47c18e6f74b33ea0747c. The solution file is ContosoApp.sln with src/ and test/ directories.

Perform a thorough review focusing on:
1. Duplicated properties across project files (TargetFramework, LangVersion, Nullable, ImplicitUsings, etc.)
2. Missing or improvable Directory.Build.props / Directory.Build.targets
3. Package version management — should Central Package Management (CPM) be used?
4. Anti-patterns (hardcoded paths, restated SDK defaults, Exec instead of built-in tasks, unquoted conditions, etc.)
5. Modernization opportunities (legacy format, unnecessary explicit Compile includes, etc.)
6. Any other MSBuild best practices violations

For each finding, provide:
- What the issue is
- Where it occurs (file paths)
- The recommended fix with concrete before/after examples

Start by discovering all relevant files, then read and analyze each one.

data.content:
Now I have complete visibility into the solution. Let me compile the full review.

---

# MSBuild Project Review: ContosoApp Solution

## 📋 Solution Overview

| Item | Details |
|---|---|
| **Solution** | `ContosoApp.sln` — 5 projects |
| **Source projects** | `ContosoApp.Core`, `ContosoApp.Services`, `ContosoApp.Api` |
| **Test projects** | `ContosoApp.Core.Tests`, `ContosoApp.Api.Tests` |
| **Format** | All SDK-style ✅ |
| **Directory.Build.props** | ❌ Missing |
| **Directory.Build.targets** | ❌ Missing |
| **Directory.Packages.props** | ❌ Missing (no CPM) |
| **global.json** | ❌ Missing |

---

## 🔴 Errors (Likely to cause bugs or inconsistent behavior)

### E1 — Package version drift on `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions`

The same package is referenced at **two different versions** across projects that depend on each other:

| Project | Version |
|---|---|
| `src/ContosoApp.Core/ContosoApp.Core.csproj` | **8.0.0** |
| `src/ContosoApp.Services/ContosoApp.Services.csproj` | **8.0.1** |
| `src/ContosoApp.Api/ContosoApp.Api.csproj` | **8.0.0** |

`ContosoApp.Services` references `ContosoApp.Core` (which brings in 8.0.0 transitively), yet explicitly requests 8.0.1. `ContosoApp.Api` references both — NuGet's resolver will silently pick the highest (8.0.1) at runtime, but the `.csproj` files tell different stories. This version incoherence gets worse over time and is a maintenance hazard.

**Fix:** Adopt Central Package Management (see S1 below) or at minimum align all three to the same version.

---

### E2 — Test infrastructure package version skew

The two test projects use **different versions** of their shared test dependencies:

| Package | `Core.Tests` | `Api.Tests` |
|---|---|---|
| `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` | **17.10.0** | **17.9.0** |
| `xunit` | **2.8.1** | **2.7.1** |
| `xunit.runner.visualstudio` | **2.8.1** | **2.5.6** |

This can lead to subtle test behavior differences between projects (different assertion semantics, test discovery bugs, etc.) and makes coordinated upgrades painful.

**Fix:** Centralize versions via CPM (see S1).

---

## 🟡 Warnings (Anti-patterns that should be fixed)

### W1 — Massive property duplication across all 5 projects (→ needs `Directory.Build.props`)

**All 5 projects** contain this identical block:

```xml
<TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
<Nullable>enable</Nullable>
<ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
<TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
<EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
<Company>Contoso</Company>
<Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
```

That's **7 properties × 5 files = 35 duplicated lines**. When you need to change the target framework to `net9.0` or update the company name, you'll need to touch 5 files. This violates DRY and is the #1 reason `Directory.Build.props` exists.

**Fix:** Create a root-level `Directory.Build.props`:

```xml
<!-- /Directory.Build.props -->
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <Company>Contoso</Company>
    <Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

Then **remove** all 7 properties from every `.csproj`.

---

### W2 — Duplicated `DocumentationFile` property across 3 source projects

All three `src/` projects have a second `<PropertyGroup>` containing:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <DocumentationFile>$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml</DocumentationFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

This is duplicated in `ContosoApp.Core.csproj`, `ContosoApp.Services.csproj`, and `ContosoApp.Api.csproj`.

**Fix:** Add to `Directory.Build.props` with a condition that excludes test projects:

```xml
<!-- In Directory.Build.props -->
<PropertyGroup Condition="!$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests'))">
  <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

> **Note:** Use the built-in `<GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>` instead of manually setting `<DocumentationFile>`. The SDK computes the correct output path automatically. The manual `$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml` is a restated SDK default when `GenerateDocumentationFile` is true.

---

### W3 — Duplicated `PrintBuildInfo` target across 3 source projects (→ needs `Directory.Build.targets`)

All three `src/` projects contain this identical custom target:

```xml
<Target Name="PrintBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="Build">
  <Message Importance="high" Text="Building $(AssemblyName) [$(Configuration)]" />
</Target>
```

**Fix:** Create `Directory.Build.targets` at the repo root:

```xml
<!-- /Directory.Build.targets -->
<Project>
  <Target Name="PrintBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="Build"
          Condition="!$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests'))">
    <Message Importance="high" Text="Building $(AssemblyName) [$(Configuration)]" />
  </Target>
</Project>
```

Then **remove** the `<Target>` block from all three `.csproj` files.

---

### W4 — `IsPackable` only set on test projects, not on `src/` libraries

Both test projects correctly have `<IsPackable>false</IsPackable>`, but none of the source projects explicitly declare their packability. If `ContosoApp.Core` or `ContosoApp.Services` are internal-only libraries that should never be published as NuGet packages, a missing `IsPackable` flag means `dotnet pack` will happily create packages.

**Fix:** Add to `Directory.Build.props`:

```xml
<!-- Default all projects to not packable, then opt-in selectively -->
<PropertyGroup>
  <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
</PropertyGroup>
```

Then remove `<IsPackable>false</IsPackable>` from the two test `.csproj` files, and add `<IsPackable>true</IsPackable>` only to any project that should be packaged.

---

### W5 — Unnecessary `<PropertyGroup>` fragmentation

`ContosoApp.Core.csproj`, `ContosoApp.Services.csproj`, and `ContosoApp.Api.csproj` each split properties across two separate `<PropertyGroup>` elements for no reason (the second one only contains `DocumentationFile`). Once W2 is resolved by moving to `Directory.Build.props`, this fragmentation goes away automatically.

---

## 🔵 Suggestions (Improvements for maintainability and best practices)

### S1 — Adopt Central Package Management (CPM)

With 5 projects and 8 distinct packages, the solution has reached the complexity where CPM pays for itself. Currently, versions are scattered across individual `.csproj` files, leading to drift (see E1, E2).

**Fix:** Create `Directory.Packages.props` at the repo root:

```xml
<!-- /Directory.Packages.props -->
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <!-- Shared -->
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" Version="8.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.3" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Swashbuckle.AspNetCore" Version="6.5.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="System.Text.Json" Version="8.0.5" />

    <!-- Test -->
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.10.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="NSubstitute" Version="5.1.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit" Version="2.8.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.8.1" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>
```

Then **remove `Version="..."` attributes** from all `<PackageReference>` items in every `.csproj`. For example, `ContosoApp.Core.csproj` becomes:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" />
  <PackageReference Include="System.Text.Json" />
</ItemGroup>
```

---

### S2 — Add `global.json` to pin the SDK version

Without a `global.json`, different developers or CI agents may use different SDK versions, leading to inconsistent builds.

**Fix:** Create at the repo root:

```json
{
  "sdk": {
    "version": "8.0.300",
    "rollForward": "latestFeature"
  }
}
```

---

### S3 — Redundant transitive `System.Text.Json` reference in `ContosoApp.Api`

`ContosoApp.Api` explicitly references `System.Text.Json` version 8.0.5, but it already gets this transitively through its `ProjectReference` to `ContosoApp.Core` (which also references `System.Text.Json` 8.0.5). The explicit reference in `Api` is redundant.

**Where:** `src/ContosoApp.Api/ContosoApp.Api.csproj`, line 17

**Fix:** Remove the redundant reference:
```xml
<!-- BEFORE (ContosoApp.Api.csproj) -->
<ItemGroup>
  <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" Version="8.0.0" />
  <PackageReference Include="Swashbuckle.AspNetCore" Version="6.5.0" />
  <PackageReference Include="System.Text.Json" Version="8.0.5" />    <!-- redundant -->
</ItemGroup>

<!-- AFTER -->
<ItemGroup>
  <PackageReference Include="Swashbuckle.AspNetCore" Version="6.5.0" />
</ItemGroup>
```

> Similarly, `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions` in `ContosoApp.Api` is transitively available through both `Core` and `Services`. If you adopt CPM, keeping it explicit is harmless, but without CPM it's another surface for version drift.

---

### S4 — Add `PrivateAssets="all"` to `xunit.runner.visualstudio`

The `xunit.runner.visualstudio` package is test infrastructure that should never flow as a transitive dependency. While the test projects already have `IsPackable=false` (so it won't escape via NuGet), best practice is to mark it:

```xml
<PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.8.1">
  <PrivateAssets>all</PrivateAssets>
  <IncludeAssets>runtime; build; native; contentfiles; analyzers; buildtransitive</IncludeAssets>
</PackageReference>
```

### E1 — Package version drift on `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions`

| Project | Version |
|---|---|
| `src/ContosoApp.Core/ContosoApp.Core.csproj` | **8.0.0** |
| `src/ContosoApp.Services/ContosoApp.Services.csproj` | **8.0.1** |
| `src/ContosoApp.Api/ContosoApp.Api.csproj` | **8.0.0** |

`ContosoApp.Services` references `ContosoApp.Core` (which brings in 8.0.0 transitively), yet explicitly requests 8.0.1. `ContosoApp.Api` references both — NuGet's resolver will silently pick the highest (8.0.1) at runtime, but the `.csproj` files tell different stories. This version incoherence gets worse over time and is a maintenance hazard.

**Fix:** Adopt Central Package Management (see S1 below) or at minimum align all three to the same version.

---

### E2 — Test infrastructure package version skew

The two test projects use **different versions** of their shared test dependencies:

| Package | `Core.Tests` | `Api.Tests` |
|---|---|---|
| `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` | **17.10.0** | **17.9.0** |
| `xunit` | **2.8.1** | **2.7.1** |
| `xunit.runner.visualstudio` | **2.8.1** | **2.5.6** |

This can lead to subtle test behavior differences between projects (different assertion semantics, test discovery bugs, etc.) and makes coordinated upgrades painful.

**Fix:** Centralize versions via CPM (see S1).

**All 5 projects** contain this identical block:

```xml
<TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
<Nullable>enable</Nullable>
<ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
<TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
<EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
<Company>Contoso</Company>
<Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
```

That's **7 properties × 5 files = 35 duplicated lines**. When you need to change the target framework to `net9.0` or update the company name, you'll need to touch 5 files. This violates DRY and is the #1 reason `Directory.Build.props` exists.

**Fix:** Create a root-level `Directory.Build.props`:

```xml
<!-- /Directory.Build.props -->
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <Company>Contoso</Company>
    <Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

Then **remove** all 7 properties from every `.csproj`.

All three `src/` projects have a second `<PropertyGroup>` containing:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <DocumentationFile>$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml</DocumentationFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

This is duplicated in `ContosoApp.Core.csproj`, `ContosoApp.Services.csproj`, and `ContosoApp.Api.csproj`.

**Fix:** Add to `Directory.Build.props` with a condition that excludes test projects:

```xml
<!-- In Directory.Build.props -->
<PropertyGroup Condition="!$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests'))">
  <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

> **Note:** Use the built-in `<GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>` instead of manually setting `<DocumentationFile>`. The SDK computes the correct output path automatically. The manual `$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml` is a restated SDK default when `GenerateDocumentationFile` is true.

```xml
<Target Name="PrintBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="Build">
  <Message Importance="high" Text="Building $(AssemblyName) [$(Configuration)]" />
</Target>
```

**Fix:** Create `Directory.Build.targets` at the repo root:

```xml
<!-- /Directory.Build.targets -->
<Project>
  <Target Name="PrintBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="Build"
          Condition="!$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests'))">
    <Message Importance="high" Text="Building $(AssemblyName) [$(Configuration)]" />
  </Target>
</Project>
```

Then **remove** the `<Target>` block from all three `.csproj` files.

Both test projects correctly have `<IsPackable>false</IsPackable>`, but none of the source projects explicitly declare their packability. </PropertyGroup>

This version incoherence gets worse over time and is a maintenance hazard.

**Fix:** Adopt Central Package Management (see S1 below) or at minimum align all three to the same version.

---

### E2 — Test infrastructure package version skew

The two test projects use **different versions** of their shared test dependencies:

| Package | `Core.Tests` | `Api.Tests` |
|---|---|---|
| `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` | **17.10.0** | **17.9.0** |
| `xunit` | **2.8.1** | **2.7.1** |
| `xunit.runner.visualstudio` | **2.8.1** | **2.5.6** |

This can lead to subtle test behavior differences between projects (different assertion semantics, test discovery bugs, etc.) and makes coordinated upgrades painful.

**Fix:** Centralize versions via CPM (see S1).

---

## 🟡 Warnings (Anti-patterns that should be fixed)

### W1 — Massive property duplication across all 5 projects (→ needs `Directory.Build.props`)

**All 5 projects** contain this identical block:

```xml
<TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
<Nullable>enable</Nullable>
<ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
<TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
<EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
<Company>Contoso</Company>
<Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
```

That's **7 properties × 5 files = 35 duplicated lines**. When you need to change the target framework to `net9.0` or update the company name, you'll need to touch 5 files. This violates DRY and is the #1 reason `Directory.Build.props` exists.

**Fix:** Create a root-level `Directory.Build.props`:

```xml
<!-- /Directory.Build.props -->
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <Company>Contoso</Company>
    <Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

Then **remove** all 7 properties from every `.csproj`.

---

### W2 — Duplicated `DocumentationFile` property across 3 source projects

All three `src/` projects have a second `<PropertyGroup>` containing:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <DocumentationFile>$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml</DocumentationFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

This is duplicated in `ContosoApp.Core.csproj`, `ContosoApp.Services.csproj`, and `ContosoApp.Api.csproj`.

**Fix:** Add to `Directory.Build.props` with a condition that excludes test projects:

```xml
<!-- In Directory.Build.props -->
<PropertyGroup Condition="!$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests'))">
  <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

> **Note:** Use the built-in `<GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>` instead of manually setting `<DocumentationFile>`. The SDK computes the correct output path automatically. The manual `$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml` is a restated SDK default when `GenerateDocumentationFile` is true.

---

### W3 — Duplicated `PrintBuildInfo` target across 3 source projects (→ needs `Directory.Build.targets`)

All three `src/` projects contain this identical custom target:

```xml
<Target Name="PrintBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="Build">
  <Message Importance="high" Text="Building $(AssemblyName) [$(Configuration)]" />
</Target>
```

**Fix:** Create `Directory.Build.targets` at the repo root:

```xml
<!-- /Directory.Build.targets -->
<Project>
  <Target Name="PrintBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="Build"
          Condition="!$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests'))">
    <Message Importance="high" Text="Building $(AssemblyName) [$(Configuration)]" />
  </Target>
</Project>
```

Then **remove** the `<Target>` block from all three `.csproj` files.

---

### W4 — `IsPackable` only set on test projects, not on `src/` libraries

Both test projects correctly have `<IsPackable>false</IsPackable>`, but none of the source projects explicitly declare their packability. If `ContosoApp.Core` or `ContosoApp.Services` are internal-only libraries that should never be published as NuGet packages, a missing `IsPackable` flag means `dotnet pack` will happily create packages.

**Fix:** Add to `Directory.Build.props`:

```xml
<!-- Default all projects to not packable, then opt-in selectively -->
<PropertyGroup>
  <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
</PropertyGroup>
```

Then remove `<IsPackable>false</IsPackable>` from the two test `.csproj` files, and add `<IsPackable>true</IsPackable>` only to any project that should be packaged.

---

### W5 — Unnecessary `<PropertyGroup>` fragmentation

`ContosoApp.Core.csproj`, `ContosoApp.Services.csproj`, and `ContosoApp.Api.csproj` each split properties across two separate `<PropertyGroup>` elements for no reason (the second one only contains `DocumentationFile`). Once W2 is resolved by moving to `Directory.Build.props`, this fragmentation goes away automatically.

---

## 🔵 Suggestions (Improvements for maintainability and best practices)

### S1 — Adopt Central Package Management (CPM)

With 5 projects and 8 distinct packages, the solution has reached the complexity where CPM pays for itself. Currently, versions are scattered across individual `.csproj` files, leading to drift (see E1, E2).

**Fix:** Create `Directory.Packages.props` at the repo root:

```xml
<!-- /Directory.Packages.props -->
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <!-- Shared -->
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" Version="8.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.3" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Swashbuckle.AspNetCore" Version="6.5.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="System.Text.Json" Version="8.0.5" />

    <!-- Test -->
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.10.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="NSubstitute" Version="5.1.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit" Version="2.8.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.8.1" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>
```

Then **remove `Version="..."` attributes** from all `<PackageReference>` items in every `.csproj`. For example, `ContosoApp.Core.csproj` becomes:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" />
  <PackageReference Include="System.Text.Json" />
</ItemGroup>
```

---

### S2 — Add `global.json` to pin the SDK version

Without a `global.json`, different developers or CI agents may use different SDK versions, leading to inconsistent builds.

**Fix:** Create at the repo root:

```json
{
  "sdk": {
    "version": "8.0.300",
    "rollForward": "latestFeature"
  }
}
```

---

### S3 — Redundant transitive `System.Text.Json` reference in `ContosoApp.Api`

`ContosoApp.Api` explicitly references `System.Text.Json` version 8.0.5, but it already gets this transitively through its `ProjectReference` to `ContosoApp.Core` (which also references `System.Text.Json` 8.0.5). The explicit reference in `Api` is redundant.

**Where:** `src/ContosoApp.Api/ContosoApp.Api.csproj`, line 17

**Fix:** Remove the redundant reference:
```xml
<!-- BEFORE (ContosoApp.Api.csproj) -->
<ItemGroup>
  <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" Version="8.0.0" />
  <PackageReference Include="Swashbuckle.AspNetCore" Version="6.5.0" />
  <PackageReference Include="System.Text.Json" Version="8.0.5" />    <!-- redundant -->
</ItemGroup>

<!-- AFTER -->
<ItemGroup>
  <PackageReference Include="Swashbuckle.AspNetCore" Version="6.5.0" />
</ItemGroup>
```

> Similarly, `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions` in `ContosoApp.Api` is transitively available through both `Core` and `Services`. If you adopt CPM, keeping it explicit is harmless, but without CPM it's another surface for version drift.

---

### S4 — Add `PrivateAssets="all"` to `xunit.runner.visualstudio`

The `xunit.runner.visualstudio` package is test infrastructure that should never flow as a transitive dependency. While the test projects already have `IsPackable=false` (so it won't escape via NuGet), best practice is to mark it:

```xml
<PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.8.1">
  <PrivateAssets>all</PrivateAssets>
  <IncludeAssets>runtime; build; native; contentfiles; analyzers; buildtransitive</IncludeAssets>
</PackageReference>
```

**Where:** `test/ContosoApp.Core.Tests/ContosoApp.Core.Tests.csproj` (line 17) and `test/ContosoApp.Api.Tests/ContosoApp.Api.Tests.csproj` (line 17).

---

## 🟢 Positive (Good practices already in place)

| Practice | Notes |
|---|---|
| ✅ **All SDK-style projects** | No legacy `<Import Project="$(MSBuildToolsPath)..."` — already modern format |
| ✅ **No `packages.config`** | All using `PackageReference` |
| ✅ **No explicit `<Compile Include="...">`** | Relying on SDK implicit globbing correctly |
| ✅ **`TreatWarningsAsErrors` enabled everywhere** | Enforces code quality |
| ✅ **`EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` enabled** | Style rules run during build |
| ✅ **Test projects marked `IsPackable=false`** | Prevents accidental packaging |
| ✅ **No hardcoded paths** | All paths use MSBuild properties (`$(OutputPath)`, etc.) |
| ✅ **No `Exec` anti-pattern** | Uses built-in `<Message>` task correctly |
| ✅ **Clean project references** | Correct relative paths, no circular dependencies |

---
